retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: For instance, poor specimen quality is the first sign leading to an erroneous result. A perfectly carried out inoculation to the Mueller-Hinton agar plate using mixed colonies yields unsatisfactory results. Poor standardization of bacterial suspension and a “longer depth of agar” could yield misleading endpoints. Supervision for laboratory staff is necessary to prevent wasting time and resources. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Purchasing poor-quality MIC panels can lead to “dehydrated wells” or “mixed wells.” Completing the procedure without personal protective equipment can increase the incidence of laboratory-acquired infections. [27] Inadequacy and lack of supplies will extend turn-around time, decreasing laboratory productivity and delaying patient therapy. [28] Completing the testing for antibiotics that do not align with the hospital’s formulary makes laboratory service available yet ineffective. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Sputum Analysis -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Quality assurance is crucial for differentiating actual respiratory pathogens from potential colonizing flora. The quality of commercial laboratory products—such as culture plates, staining kits, and molecular biology kits—must meet the approval standards set by regulatory bodies, including the United States Food and Drug Administration, the Public Health Agency of Canada, and similar agencies in Europe and Australia. Substandard products or expired materials can compromise test performance. Several agencies determine the quality of laboratory work, and the College of American Pathologists plays a significant role in establishing laboratory standards and quality control procedures, which are essential for avoiding various interfering factors.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 3: Use of Engineering and Work Practice Controls to Reduce the Opportunity for Patient and Healthcare Worker Exposure to Potentially Infectious Material
retrieved_docs.content: Antimicrobial stewardship programs help control antimicrobial resistance, improve outcomes, and reduce healthcare costs. Antimicrobial stewardship should be programmed to monitor antimicrobial susceptibility profiles, anticipating and assessing any new antimicrobial resistance patterns. These trends should be correlated with the antimicrobial agents used to assess susceptibility patterns. [28] Antimicrobial stewardship programs can be designed as either active or passive initiatives, targeting either pre-prescription or post-prescription periods. An active program includes prescription restrictions and preauthorization in the pre-prescription period, whereas passive initiatives include education, guidelines, and antimicrobial susceptibility reports. An active post-prescription program focuses on providing real-time feedback to clinicians regarding antibiotic usage, dose, bioavailability, and susceptibility, with automatic conversion of intravenous to oral formulations. In contrast, a passive post-prescription program involves integrating electronic medical records to generate alerts for prolonged prescriptions and mismatches between antibiotics and microorganisms. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic, Chemical, and Radiation Terrorism Review -- Issues of Concern -- Biologic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Signs/Symptoms: In humans expected development would be over 6 to 24 hours, a refined version might act quicker, it would quickly cause devasting neurologic signs and symptoms and might result in sudden death. If weaponized, it would probably be dispersed by aerosolizing. Treatment: There has been very little progress toward the assembly of a good human immunogen against C. perfringens or any type of anti-toxin, and within the event of a terrorist attack, no prophylactic measures would possibly be accessible to be used by the general public. Biologic Warfare: C. perfringens epsilon toxin could be a fatal bioterrorism weapon. If used effectively, this agent might cause important morbidity and mortality because of a lack of therapeutic or preventive measures exist as medical countermeasures. retrieved_docs.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Several factors affect the result of the susceptibility testing, which covers the whole sampling, testing, and reporting procedures. Any deviation from the standard antimicrobial susceptibility testing procedure can significantly impact succeeding areas of laboratory workflow, which could later affect patient diagnosis, treatment, and management. The laboratory workflow support systems require strict monitoring, and laboratory personnel should be well-trained and competent to perform the procedure. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Antibiotics -- Function -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: The pharmacology behind antibiotics includes destroying the bacterial cell by either preventing cell reproduction or changing a necessary cellular function or process within the cell. Antimicrobial agents are classically grouped into two main categories based on their in vitro effect on bacteria: bactericidal and bacteriostatic. Common teaching often explains that bactericidal antibiotics "kill" bacteria and bacteriostatic antibiotics "prevent the growth" of bacteria. The true definition is not so simple. To accurately define each category, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) must be understood. The lowest concentration that inhibits visible bacterial growth at 24 hours is the MIC. [2] The MBC is the concentration of an antibiotic that reduces bacterial density by 1000-fold at 24 hours. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1388
retrieved_docs.content: Biguanides and amidines as antiseptics and disinfectants, nitrofuran derivatives, phenol and derivatives, and quaternary ammonium compounds are all distinct chemical classes that belong to the broader category of antiseptics and disinfectants. Each of these agents is used to eliminate or inhibit the growth of microorganisms on skin, surfaces, and medical equipment, providing essential antimicrobial protection in clinical and environmental settings. Although they differ in chemical structure and specific spectrum of activity, recognizing that they all function as antiseptics and disinfectants helps clinicians and infection‑control professionals select the most appropriate agent for a given patient‑care or hygiene application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 456
retrieved_docs.content: Kirby‑Bauer bacterial sensitivity is a standardized disk diffusion assay employed to evaluate the susceptibility of bacterial isolates to antimicrobial agents. In this testing method, antibiotic-impregnated disks containing agents such as chlortetracyklin and framycetin are placed on an agar surface inoculated with the target organism. The resulting zones of inhibition indicate the effectiveness of chlortetracyklin, a tetracycline‑derived compound, and framycetin, an aminoglycoside, against the tested bacteria. By providing clear, quantitative data on how these specific antibiotics inhibit bacterial growth, the Kirby‑Bauer bacterial sensitivity test guides clinicians in selecting appropriate antimicrobial therapy and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1867
retrieved_docs.content: Phenol and its derivatives and quaternary ammonium compounds are both classified as types of antiseptics and disinfectants, belonging to the broader category of agents used to inhibit or destroy microorganisms on living tissues and inanimate surfaces. As members of the antiseptics and disinfectants group, these chemical families share the common purpose of preventing infection and maintaining hygiene in clinical and community settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1423
retrieved_docs.content: Ciprofloxacin and demethylchlortetracyklin each fulfill an antibacterial therapeutic role, providing antimicrobial activity against bacterial pathogens. However, the effectiveness of both agents can be compromised when a unicellular infection (عدوى وحيدات الخلية) is present, as such infections may prevent the drugs from achieving their intended antibacterial effect. Recognizing the potential interference of unicellular infections is important for optimizing the use of ciprofloxacin and demethylchlortetracyklin in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment constitute a therapeutic category aimed at managing infections within the mouth, and this category includes active agents such as natamycin and domiphen. Natamycin, an antifungal compound, is incorporated into oral formulations to inhibit the growth of fungal pathogens, while domiphen, a quaternary ammonium antiseptic, contributes antimicrobial activity by disrupting microbial cell membranes. Together, these ingredients provide a combined antifungal and antiseptic effect that enhances the efficacy of locally applied oral treatments for preventing and controlling oral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2715
retrieved_docs.content: Minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) is a quantitative measure that defines the lowest concentration of an antimicrobial agent required to kill a bacterial population, and it is directly related to the antibiotic framycetin. Understanding the MBC of framycetin helps clinicians assess the drug’s bactericidal potency, guide appropriate dosing, and evaluate its effectiveness against specific bacterial infections, thereby informing optimal patient care and antimicrobial stewardship.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 366
retrieved_docs.content: Acetarsol and phenoxyethanol are both classified as antiinfective substances, indicating that each compound functions as an antimicrobial agent. This shared categorization highlights their role in inhibiting or eliminating infectious microorganisms, making them relevant in therapeutic contexts where control of infection is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2506
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics for systemic use are linked to the class of tuberculostatic antibiotics (antibiotika tuberculostatická), indicating that systemic antimicrobial therapy includes agents specifically designed to inhibit the growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis. This relationship highlights that tuberculostatic antibiotics are a subset of systemic antibiotics used in the treatment of tuberculosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrofuran derivatives, Other antiseptics and disinfectants in ATC, Phenol and derivatives, and Quaternary ammonium compounds are all classified as antiseptics and disinfectants. These four chemical groups represent distinct subclasses within the broader antiseptic and disinfectant category, each used to prevent or eliminate microbial contamination in clinical, industrial, and environmental settings. While they share the common purpose of infection control, they differ in their chemical structures and specific antimicrobial spectra.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2079
retrieved_docs.content: Biguanides and amidines, phenol and its derivatives, and quaternary ammonium compounds are all distinct chemical classes that belong to the broader category of antiseptics and disinfectants. Each of these groups functions as an antiseptic or disinfectant, meaning they are employed to reduce or eliminate microbial contamination on living tissues or inanimate surfaces, thereby helping to prevent infection. While their chemical structures and mechanisms of action differ, they share the common purpose of contributing to infection control in medical and hygiene settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics for systemic use encompass a group of tuberculostatic antibiotics, referred to in Czech as “antibiotika tuberkulostatická.” This subclass of systemic antibiotics is specifically employed to inhibit the growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis and is often used in multidrug‑resistant or refractory tuberculosis regimens. Within the tuberculostatic category, both cykloserin (cycloserine) and viomycin are recognized agents; each exemplifies a distinct chemical type that shares the common therapeutic purpose of tuberculostatic activity. By incorporating cykloserin and viomycin, the broader class of antibiotics for systemic use provides clinicians with alternative options for targeting persistent mycobacterial infections, thereby enhancing the flexibility and effectiveness of tuberculosis treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 79
retrieved_docs.content: agents integrate quinoline derivatives, cationic peptides, beta‑lactam antibiotics, quaternary ammonium compounds, and other chemical classes under a unified therapeutic framework, with specific formulations (e.g., throat antiseptics) drawing on multiple members of these families to provide comprehensive antimicrobial efficacy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 537
retrieved_docs.content: IIS‑PAA is classified as an antibacterial agent, as are ceftazidim and gramicidin, indicating that these compounds share the primary function of inhibiting bacterial growth. Within the broader antibacterial agent category, domiphen and sulfapyridine are identified as subordinate or child subclasses, reflecting more specific types of antibacterial substances. The antibacterial agent class is also linked to therapeutic classes exemplified by lefamulin acetate and rifampin, suggesting that these agents belong to or are associated with distinct therapeutic groups used in antimicrobial treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1651
retrieved_docs.content: The antibacterial therapeutic role is played by ciprofloxacin, indicating that ciprofloxacin functions as an antibacterial agent used to treat bacterial infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 462
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotic throat preparations are a specific category of throat preparations that are formulated to deliver antimicrobial agents directly to the oropharyngeal region. Within this class, fusafungin and gramicidin are recognized as constituent members, providing the antibacterial activity characteristic of these preparations. By being classified as throat preparations, antibiotic throat preparations are intended for topical use in the mouth and throat to treat or prevent bacterial infections, leveraging the therapeutic properties of fusafungin and gramicidin to achieve effective local antimicrobial effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1517
retrieved_docs.content: Micronomicin is classified as an aminoglycoside antibacterial, placing it within the aminoglycoside antibacterials class of antibiotics that inhibit bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 30 S ribosomal subunit. This categorization identifies micronomicin as a member of a drug group commonly used to treat serious Gram‑negative infections.
